codeReview.summary: LOCK-ORDERING.md is comprehensive and accurate. Independently verified all 13 IrqMutex instances via grep — the document captures every one with correct file locations and line numbers. Verified key multi-lock code paths against source: (1) two-phase wake pattern in channel.rs and timer.rs correctly describes lock-release-lock sequence, (2) slab→page_allocator nesting confirmed in slab::try_alloc()→SlabCache::alloc()→grow()→page_allocator::alloc_frame(), (3) scheduler→page_allocator nesting confirmed in maybe_cleanup_killed_process(), (4) KERNEL_PT_LOCK→page_allocator nesting confirmed in memory.rs, (5) exit_current_from_syscall multi-phase sequential pattern verified — scheduler lock released before address space cleanup. The circular dependency analysis table correctly verifies no reverse edges exist. Interrupt safety analysis is sound — all locks use IrqMutex which masks IRQs, preventing reentry. No bare Mutex types exist in the kernel. The discoveredIssues suggestion about deferring address space cleanup outside the scheduler lock in maybe_cleanup_killed_process is a valid optimization observation, correctly classified as a suggestion rather than a bug.

issues.description: Level numbering inconsistency: The document states 'A lock at level N may only acquire locks at level N+1 or higher' but then KERNEL_PT_LOCK (level 3) acquires page_allocator (level 2), and slab (level 2) acquires page_allocator (level 2). Both cases are correctly explained in prose as safe (no reverse edges), but the level numbering table itself contradicts the stated rule. Consider either: (a) renumbering so KERNEL_PT_LOCK is below page_allocator, or (b) rephrasing the rule to 'the DAG is authoritative; levels are approximate guides' upfront rather than noting exceptions inline.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The kernel-auditor skill's TDD requirement is ambiguous for documentation-only features. The worker correctly noted (in skillFeedback.suggestedChanges) that the skill's 'Special Cases' section for cross-file invariant features doesn't explicitly state that no new tests are expected when no bugs are found. The existing text says 'trace specific cross-cutting concerns across all relevant files. Create a document or code comments mapping the invariants' but doesn't address TDD expectations. The worker's judgment to skip tests was correct since no code bugs were found.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'The kernel-auditor skill's Special Cases section for cross-file invariant features could explicitly mention that no new tests are expected when the audit finds no bugs — it's currently ambiguous whether the TDD requirement applies to documentation-only features.'

sharedStateObservations.observation: No services.yaml file exists in the repo (.factory/services.yaml). The AGENTS.md references it for build/test/stress commands but the file is missing. Workers have been running commands without it.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Glob for .factory/services.yaml returned no results. AGENTS.md says 'See .factory/services.yaml for build/test/stress commands.'

summary: Pass. LOCK-ORDERING.md is a thorough and accurate cross-file lock ordering analysis. All 13 IrqMutex instances are correctly catalogued. The lock ordering DAG, 12 multi-lock code paths, interrupt safety analysis, and circular dependency verification were all independently verified against the kernel source. One non-blocking documentation issue: the level numbering scheme has minor inconsistencies that are addressed in prose but could be cleaner. No code bugs found, no tests needed. The worker followed the skill procedure correctly.
